landing.title: Match your vote on local government
landing.subtitle: 30 questions on service delivery, housing, transport, safety, and coalition governance. See which parties align with your priorities for the 2026 Local Government Elections.
landing.privacy: Your answers stay on this device. No tracking, no accounts.

howItHelps.title: Why this matters now
howItHelps.intro: Service delivery, audit outcomes, housing, coalitions, transport, safety. Compare parties on the issues that shape daily life in South Africa's municipalities.

benefit1.title: Look past slogans
benefit1.text: See actual positions on service delivery, housing, audit culture, coalition deals, and transport — not just campaign posters.

benefit2.title: Spot real options
benefit2.text: Find parties you may not follow, based on local policy fit instead of social media reach.

benefit3.title: Read the field
benefit3.text: Get a ranked view of every party so you can see where they bunch together and where they split on local issues.

benefit4.title: Walk into the booth ready
benefit4.text: Decide with clarity before you queue. Save or share a link to your own answers.

high.title: Clear separation
high.description: Your top match stands well ahead of alternatives. The gap shows a distinct alignment.

medium.title: Close clustering
medium.description: Your top matches are within a few points of each other. Several parties share similar positions on your priorities.

low.title: Tight spread
low.description: Many parties score similarly. The differences are marginal; dig deeper into specific axes that matter to you.

about.title: About Elekti
about.methodologyText: Elekti is rooted in South African policy documents, municipal governance records, and public statements. We score across 10 issue areas specific to local government. The maths is deterministic and transparent.
about.howItWorksText: 30 questions cover service delivery, financial accountability, housing and informal settlements, rates and tariffs, coalition governance, public transport, land use and densification, local policing, community participation, and local economic development. Each question links to an issue axis. You answer, we map your stance, and we compare it to each party's stated position.
about.scoringText: For every axis we calculate similarity between your answers and each party, weight by question importance, normalise by the questions you answered, then average across all axes. Scores are percentages; confidence reflects how clear the gap is between your top match and the rest.
about.transparencyText: All positions, weights, and calculations live in the repo. If something is wrong, send sources and we'll update. This tool is independent and does not promote any party.
about.dataSourcesText: We rely on manifestos, policy documents, council voting records, Auditor-General reports, and verified public statements. When positions change, we update.
about.privacyText: Your answers never leave your device. No accounts, no identifiers.
about.noTrackingText: We don't use cookies or pixels that follow you. We cannot see who you are or what you answered.
about.localOnlyText: Everything is saved in your browser. Clear your data and it's gone; we have nothing on our side.
about.sharingText: Sharing creates a URL that encodes your answers. Anyone with the link can view your result. Remove the link to keep it private again.
about.analyticsText: We use privacy-first, cookie-free analytics only to see if people are visiting. They do not record your answers.
about.ballotText: In local government elections you receive two ballot papers. Your Elekti result is most relevant to the party ballot.
about.wardBallotText: You vote for a ward councillor — an individual candidate who will represent your specific ward. Candidates may stand for a party or as independents. Consider the person, not only the party.
about.prBallotText: You vote for a party. This determines proportional representation seats on the council and, indirectly, which party controls the majority and who becomes mayor or speaker. This is the vote your Elekti match speaks to.
about.partiesText: We list major parties contesting in South Africa's local municipalities. If we missed one, point us to their published policies.
about.disclaimerText: Use this as a starting point. Read manifestos, attend ward committee meetings, and test our data. Politics shifts; so should your judgement.
about.viewDataText: All JSON files for questions, weights, and party positions are public. Inspect them yourself.

anc.desc: The African National Congress is South Africa's governing party, advocating for social democracy, transformation, and addressing inequality through state intervention.

da.desc: The Democratic Alliance promotes liberal democracy, free market economics, and non-racial politics with emphasis on good governance and job creation.

mk.desc: uMkhonto we Sizwe Party focuses on radical economic transformation, advocating for state-led development and addressing historical injustices.

eff.desc: The Economic Freedom Fighters is a far-left party advocating for radical economic transformation, nationalization, and land redistribution without compensation.

ifp.desc: The Inkatha Freedom Party primarily represents Zulu communities, promoting Zulu cultural values, federalism, and free market economics with social responsibility. While it has broader appeal, policy priorities centre on Zulu traditions and KwaZulu-Natal interests.

pa.desc: The Patriotic Alliance focuses on addressing immigration issues, promoting South African nationalism, and prioritising citizens' needs.

vfplus.name: Freedom Front Plus
vfplus.desc: Freedom Front Plus primarily represents Afrikaner communities and interests. The party opposes radical transformation and land expropriation, advocates for market-led economics and minimal state intervention, and defends conservative social values and traditional Afrikaner cultural preservation. It emphasises federalism and local self-determination for minority communities.

actionsa.desc: ActionSA is a centrist party focused on practical governance, anti-corruption, and economic growth through private sector engagement.

acdp.desc: The African Christian Democratic Party promotes Christian values, conservative social policies, and constitutional democracy.

cope.desc: Congress of the People advocates for merit-based, depoliticised local government, with professional municipal administration, direct accountability, and stronger ward-level democracy.

ufc.desc: Unite for Change (UFC) was formed from the merger of RISE Mzansi, BOSA, and GOOD in October 2025. It focuses on capable, merit-based local governance, anti-corruption, and universal access to basic services.

udm.desc: The United Democratic Movement is a social democratic party focused on clean governance, anti-corruption, and balanced economic policies that reward merit.

sacp.desc: The South African Communist Party advocates for socialism and workers' rights, and works toward a socialist society through state ownership of key industries.

atm.desc: The African Transformation Movement advocates for conservative social values, stricter discipline in schools, and stronger law and order policing.

aljama-ah.desc: Al Jama-ah is an Islamic democratic party promoting Muslim interests, social democracy, and conservative social values while working for all South Africans.

ncc.desc: The National Coloured Congress represents Coloured community interests and advocates for addressing issues affecting the Coloured population.

pac.desc: The Pan Africanist Congress of Azania is a Black nationalist party advocating for African socialism, Pan-Africanism, and land reform based on African ownership.

q1.text: Municipal water and electricity services should be run directly by council staff rather than outsourced to private companies.

q2.text: Refuse collection, parks maintenance, and road patching should be handled by permanent council employees, not private contractors.

q3.text: Municipalities should partner with private investors through long-term concessions to upgrade and operate ageing water and sanitation infrastructure.

q4.text: Parties that receive disclaimed or adverse audit opinions for three or more consecutive years should be disqualified from leading municipal coalitions.

q5.text: Municipal managers and CFOs should face personal criminal liability and automatic dismissal for financial misconduct, without severance pay.

q6.text: Each municipality should have a dedicated anti-corruption unit, fully independent of national agencies, with power to investigate and prosecute.

q7.text: Informal settlements should be formalised in their current locations — with services and secure tenure — rather than relocated to new greenfield developments.

q8.text: Municipalities should prioritise building subsidised social rental housing near employment centres and public transport routes.

q9.text: Municipalities should enforce bylaws against land invasions quickly, even if this means evicting residents.

q10.text: Municipal tariff and rates increases should be capped at the consumer price index (CPI) each year.

q11.text: Illegal electricity and water connections in poor neighbourhoods should be regularised and subsidised by the municipality rather than disconnected.

q12.text: Property rates rebates for low-income owner-occupiers should be significantly expanded beyond current indigent thresholds.

q13.text: Coalition agreements at local government level should be fully public, legally binding, and include a mechanism for enforcing compliance.

q14.text: A party that switches coalition support mid-term without a new mandate should face a by-election in its wards.

q15.text: Motions of no confidence in a mayor should require a two-thirds majority rather than a simple majority.

q16.text: Municipalities should invest in formal public transport networks even where this disrupts existing minibus taxi routes.

q17.text: Minibus taxi associations should be formally integrated into municipal public transport networks with regulated fares and routes.

q18.text: Municipalities should invest heavily in safe cycling and pedestrian infrastructure as a primary commuter mode.

q19.text: Residential areas should be rezoned to allow higher-density development near public transport routes.

q20.text: Greenfield housing developments on the urban and town periphery should be actively deprioritised in favour of town and city centre regeneration.

q21.text: Heritage and neighbourhood-character protections should not be allowed to block higher-density development in well-located residential areas.

q22.text: Municipal law enforcement capacity should be significantly expanded, reducing reliance on SAPS for local safety.

q23.text: Community policing forums should have formal power to set local policing priorities in their area.

q24.text: CCTV surveillance networks in public spaces should be massively expanded, even if this raises privacy concerns.

q25.text: Ward committees should have binding decision-making power over a portion of their ward's capital infrastructure budget.

q26.text: Residents should be able to recall a ward councillor through a formal petition signed by 30% of registered voters.

q27.text: Developers should be required to hold binding community referendums before large residential or commercial projects are approved in a ward.

q28.text: Municipalities should actively operate business incubators and subsidised training centres to create local employment.

q29.text: Councils should give binding preference to locally owned small businesses in all municipal procurement above a minimum threshold.

q30.text: Local economic development should be led by private sector investment, with councils providing a favourable regulatory environment rather than direct intervention.

errors.invalidUrl: Invalid or corrupted results URL. Please retake the quiz.
errors.noData: No quiz data found. Please take the quiz first.
errors.loadFailed: An error occurred loading your results. Please try again.

disclaimer.title: Use this as a guide
disclaimer.educational: Elekti helps you compare parties on local government issues. It does not replace your own research, and positions can shift as coalitions and policy debates move.
disclaimer.alpha: This site is under active development. We update data as parties publish and adjust their stances.
disclaimer.research: Read manifestos, attend ward committee meetings, and review Auditor-General reports. If you spot issues in our data, send sources and we will correct them.
